---
title: My dynamic report
output: html_fragment
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, error=FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
```

```{css}
@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Orbitron:wght@400..900&display=swap');
@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Exo+2:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Orbitron:wght@400..900&display=swap');

body {
  text-align: justify;
  font-family: "Exo 2", sans-serif;
  background-color: #b1b7c1;
  margin-top: 50px;
  margin-left: 80px;
  margin-right: 80px;
}

h2 {
  text-align: center;
  font-family: "Orbitron", sans-serif;
    font-weight: bold;
    font-size: 60px;
  color: #43484c;
  margin-left: 0px
}

h3 {
  font-family: "Exo 2", sans-serif;
    font-size: 40px;
  padding-top: 40px;
  color: #43484c;
}

p {
  font-family: "Exo 2", sans-serif;
  font-size: large;
  line-height: 1.5;
  color: #ffffff
}

li {
  font-family: "Exo 2", sans-serif;
  font-size: large;
  line-height: 1.5;
  color: #ffffff
}
```

![](https://avasiaki.github.io/stats220/overwatch_logo.png)

## Match Performance Analysis

### Understanding the Data
Overwatch is a popular first-person shooter game where players use their hero abilities to work as a team, defeat the opposition, and complete an objective. Using a survey, numeric and categorical data was gathered to track my performance in Overwatch. I recorded the match duration, hero type, match outcome, game mode, eliminations, and deaths. 


Overwatch Game Modes

![](https://avasiaki.github.io/stats220/gamemode_image.png)



Overwatch Hero Types

![](https://interfaceingame.com/wp-content/uploads/overwatch-2/overwatch-2-hero-gallery-500x281.png)


### A Visual Breakdown

```{r fig.width = 8, fig.height = 4}
latest_data <- read_csv("https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/e/2PACX-1vS_rF0OiJd8elgr13B-BHTL_zzWyY0KCt0iHhhQcDdh5ImKJ-y9Zjx9wqHxLbHlAon4AikTjzq3JfZg/pub?output=csv") %>%
  rename(timestamp = 1,
         match_duration_sec = 2,
         match_outcome = 3,
         hero_type = 4,
         game_mode = 5,
         eliminations = 6,
         deaths = 7)

ggplot(data = latest_data) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = match_outcome, fill = hero_type)) +
  labs(title = "Match Outcome compared to Hero Type",
       x = "Match Outcome",
       y = "Number of Matches",
       caption = "Source: Overwatch Match Performance Data")

```



```{r fig.width = 8, fig.height = 4}

ggplot(data = latest_data) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = game_mode),
           fill = "#1A9CFF") +
  labs(title = "Matches by Game Mode",
       x = "Game Mode",
       y = "Number of Matches",
       caption = "Source: Overwatch Match Performance Data")

```



```{r fig.width = 8, fig.height = 4}

ggplot(data = latest_data) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = hero_type),
           fill = "#F77768") +
  labs(title = "Matches by Hero Type",
       x = "Hero Type",
       y = "Number of Matches",
       caption = "Source: Overwatch Match Performance Data")


```


```{r}

number_of_matches <- nrow(latest_data)

mean_eliminations <- latest_data$eliminations %>%
  mean() %>%
  round()

max_deaths <- max(latest_data$deaths)

total_eliminations <- sum(latest_data$eliminations)

## I am currently taking COMPSCI 101 and learning Python, so I am familiar with using ==. == is used to compare two objects, checking whether they are equal. 

number_of_damage <- sum(latest_data$hero_type == "Damage")
number_of_tank <- sum(latest_data$hero_type == "Tank")
number_of_support <- sum(latest_data$hero_type == "Support")

most_played_hero <- max(number_of_damage, number_of_support, number_of_tank)

```

### Some Summary Statistics
- There were `r number_of_matches` total matches recorded
- Damage was played `r number_of_damage` times, Tank selected `r number_of_tank` times, and Support chosen `r number_of_support` times
- The most played hero was played `r most_played_hero` times
- The mean number of eliminations per match was `r mean_eliminations`
- The total number of eliminations was `r total_eliminations`
- Across all matches, the maximum number of deaths was `r max_deaths`
